coredata.dc:description: Minimizing heat ingress into cryogenic storage tanks is a key challenge in thermal system design. While prior studies have examined insulation materials or baffle effects in isolation, the coupled influence of these mechanisms on overall thermal resistance remains underexplored. This study introduces a novel approach that parameterizes baffle geometry and size and couples it with insulation performance by evaluating how baffle configurations influence both internal convection and insulation resistance in a two-phase cryogenic hydrogen tank. A 2D axisymmetric model at 50 % fill level is simulated under transient conditions using commercial multiphysics simulation software, with the working fluid initially at saturation temperature and standard atmospheric pressure. The model is validated against experimental LH2 tank studies, with strong agreement in both liquid and vapor phase temperature trends. Increasing circular ring baffle diameter from 1 meter to 2 meters reduces the average Nusselt number by 16 %, velocity magnitude by 44 %, and vorticity magnitude by 17 % compared to the no-baffle case. The optimal 2 m baffle is used to assess insulation-baffle coupling, revealing that baffle inclusion increases the convective-to-conductive resistance ratio by up to 30 % at low insulation thermal conductivities but diminishes to 4 % as conductivity rises. Geometric analysis shows that curved baffles outperform rectangular designs through smoother boundary layer development, stabilizing flow, and suppressing convective transport. These findings establish that thermal performance in cryogenic tanks depends on coupled design variables, underscoring the need to jointly optimize baffle structure and insulation properties rather than treating them as isolated components.

The results show that circular ring baffles exhibit the lowest sustained velocity and vorticity magnitudes, indicating their effectiveness in suppressing convection transport. Elliptical baffles demonstrate moderate performance, while rectangular baffles maintain higher vorticity levels due to stronger localized shear effects.

Effect of baffle geometry on convective heat transfer. Time evolution of the Nusselt number averaged over the spatial domain for different baffle geometries compared to the no-baffle case. The results indicate that both the elliptical and circular ring baffles provide superior suppression of convective heat transfer across the entire time domain.

head.ce:title: Thermal analysis of baffle effects in cryogenic hydrogen tanks

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Minimizing heat ingress into cryogenic storage tanks is a key challenge in thermal system design. While prior studies have examined insulation materials or baffle effects in isolation, the coupled influence of these mechanisms on overall thermal resistance remains underexplored. This study introduces a novel approach that parameterizes baffle geometry and size and couples it with insulation performance by evaluating how baffle configurations influence both internal convection and insulation resistance in a two-phase cryogenic hydrogen tank. A 2D axisymmetric model at 50 % fill level is simulated under transient conditions using commercial multiphysics simulation software, with the working fluid initially at saturation temperature and standard atmospheric pressure. The model is validated against experimental LH2 tank studies, with strong agreement in both liquid and vapor phase temperature trends. Increasing circular ring baffle diameter from 1 meter to 2 meters reduces the average Nusselt number by 16 %, velocity magnitude by 44 %, and vorticity magnitude by 17 % compared to the no-baffle case. The optimal 2 m baffle is used to assess insulation-baffle coupling, revealing that baffle inclusion increases the convective-to-conductive resistance ratio by up to 30 % at low insulation thermal conductivities but diminishes to 4 % as conductivity rises. Geometric analysis shows that curved baffles outperform rectangular designs through smoother boundary layer development, stabilizing flow, and suppressing convective transport. These findings establish that thermal performance in cryogenic tanks depends on coupled design variables, underscoring the need to jointly optimize baffle structure and insulation properties rather than treating them as isolated components.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Hydrogen (H2) is a promising energy carrier with the potential to supplement or replace fossil fuels in the future [1]. While hydrogen possesses a high gravimetric energy density, its low volumetric energy density necessitates processing for practical storage and transport. Storage methods can be classified into physical and chemical approaches. Physically, hydrogen is stored through compression, liquefaction, or cryo-compression, while chemically, it can be bonded with carbon, oxygen, or nitrogen to form liquid organic or inorganic hydrogen carriers, or adsorbed onto metal hydrides and sorbents [2–7]. Among physical storage methods, compressed gaseous hydrogen (CGH2) is widely used, but compared to CGH2, liquid hydrogen (LH2) offers a 1.5 times higher gravimetric energy density and 2 times higher volumetric energy density [8,9]. Consequently, on an energy density basis, LH2 is a compelling option for hydrogen storage.

Thermal insulation is the primary defense against heat ingress in cryogenic hydrogen storage tanks, where even small increases in temperature can lead to vaporization and loss of stored fuel. A wide range of materials, including multi-layer insulation (MLI), aerogels, hollow microspheres, and spray-on foam, have been studied for their ability to reduce heat transfer through the tank wall [10]. In Yu et al., the authors developed a novel insulation system for large-scale LH2 tanks by integrating multiple vapor-cooled shields with multi-layer insulation and optimizing their place using genetic algorithms [11]. Swanger et al. experimentally evaluated aerogel blanket insulation systems for LH2 upper stage tanks, demonstrating their ability to reduce effective thermal conductivity and provide passive cooling through vacuum-induced gas desorption [12]. Mata et al. conducted a comparative assessment of hollow microsphere and perlite-based bulk-fill insulation for large LH2 storage tanks, emphasizing their evacuation behavior and implications for boil-off reduction [13]. Ding et al. developed a numerical model to evaluate MLI systems for LH2 tanks, incorporating spray-on polyurethane foam insulation (SOFI) alongside traditional MLI layers, with their results showing that SOFI reduces heat flux by 20.76 % compared to using MLI alone [14]. However, even high-performance insulation systems are often evaluated in isolation from internal tank dynamics, leaving open questions about how material properties interact with internal convection patterns and whether flow suppression strategies such as baffles can shift the overall thermal resistance landscape.

While insulation has been extensively studied, less attention has been paid to internal flow modification through the use of baffles. Prior studies on cryogenic tanks have explored the role of baffles primarily in relation to sloshing, pressurization, and internal convection under steady-state conditions. In Zuo et al., the authors explored different baffle configurations within a numerically modeled two-phase tank in a microgravity environment to assess its impact on the temperature profile and pressurization rate at different fill levels. The results showed a 54 % reduction in pressurization rate achieved for the optimal baffle settings [15]. In Liu et al., a numerical model assessed the impact of slosh baffles in a two-phase partially-filled tank on pressure, fluid temperature, and velocity distribution over time [16]. In Lv et al., the authors investigated a numerical model to determine the impact of baffles on the thermodynamics characteristics of liquid hydrogen under sloshing conditions, with the results indicating that baffles suppress pressure fluctuations within the liquid hydrogen reservoir but simultaneously facilitate thermal transport within the tank, increasing the pressurization rate [17]. In Haddar et al., a baffle configuration within a liquid natural gas (LNG) tank reduced the average temperature in the tank by 36 % to 44 % by reducing natural convection in a steady-state heat transfer numerical model under incompressible flow conditions [18]. In Zhang et al., the authors conducted a numerical study on sloshing behavior in liquid oxygen tanks and implemented a T-shaped baffle, which induced an anti-sloshing effect that dampens motion within the tanks [19]. Despite demonstrating benefits for sloshing and pressurization, these studies largely overlook the role of optimizing baffles to suppress transient two-phase convection and modulate thermal transport through the insulation under compressible flow conditions.

Recent efforts within NASA have converged on advanced simulation-based approaches for cryogenic thermal management, highlighting the importance of insulation configuration, pressurization schemes, and tank structure. Kartuzova et al. validated a two-phase CFD model against pressurization and sloshing experiments in NASA’s K-Site liquid hydrogen tank, capturing key thermal and mass transfer dynamics relevant for microgravity tank design and insulation strategy optimization [20]. Similarly, Bandyopadhyay et al. developed and validated a multi-node thermal-fluid model of a cryogenic liquid nitrogen tank using NASA’s Generalized Fluid System Simulation Program (GFSSP), capturing ullage stratification, wall heat conduction, and interface-driven evaporative mass transfer in good agreement with experimental data from the CRYOTE 3 test article insulated with fiberglass [21]. Perrin [22], a senior systems engineer at the NASA Marshall Space Flight Center, presented an evaluation of cryogenic propellant depot configurations, drawing on open-source NASA architecture studies to assess insulation strategies, depot functions, and the integration of cryocoolers for LH2 and LO2 storage in space-based applications. More recently, Meyer et al. documented NASA’s progress in maturing zero boil-off technologies for long-duration space missions, highlighting integrated modeling and hardware demonstrations involving high-capacity cryocoolers and distributed cooling strategies for LH2 tanks [23]. Together, these NASA-led efforts underscore the need for integrated modeling approaches that couple internal flow control with external insulation strategies.

NASA’s leadership in cryogenic thermal management has been matched by a growing international effort in the last few years. For instance, researchers at the German Aerospace Center (DLR) have investigated a purged insulation system for a reusable cryogenic tank, with the key feature being a purged gap between the cryogenic and high-temperature insulation that reduces the overall layer thickness [24]. In Kang et al., Korean researchers presented a numerical model that was developed to calculate the heat flux of a liquid hydrogen tank based on its spherical shape. The insulation thickness was also significantly reduced through the optimization of the placement of a vapor-cooling shield (VCS) on solid insulation [25]. In China, scientists have made significant strides in cryogenic research, revealing the transient heat transfer characteristics of MLI for liquid nitrogen storage tanks during precooling operations [26] and investigating the use of an advanced liquid nitrogen cooled shield (LNCS) insulation system for liquid helium storage tanks [27]. Researchers at the National Academy of Sciences in Ukraine explored screen-vacuum thermal insulation (SVTI) in order to achieve an optimal vacuum of less than
10
− 3
P a
, with the thermal conductivity consisting of 67 % conductive and 33 % radiative components [28]. Italian researchers demonstrated that fiber-reinforced aerogel blankets (FRABs) outperform perlite-based systems in insulating LNG storage/transport tanks [29]. These global initiatives underscore a shared recognition of the need for robust, scalable thermal management strategies, yet few studies to date have systematically explored the coupled influence of internal flow control features, such as baffles, with insulation design in large-scale cryogenic hydrogen tanks, a gap this work seeks to address.

While baffle design is traditionally optimized to suppress fluid motion and reduce internal heat transfer, and insulation is treated as an external boundary condition, the interplay between these two mechanisms fundamentally determines the overall thermal resistance of cryogenic tanks. This study is motivated by the need to bridge that gap through a coupled analysis, linking baffle-induced convective suppression with insulation properties to better inform integrated thermal management strategies. Building upon prior work [30], this study provides a significantly expanded investigation into the effects of baffles on transient two-phase thermofluidic behavior in cryogenic hydrogen storage tanks. First, thermofluidic simulations evaluate how circular ring baffles of varying sizes impact flow dynamics and convective heat transfer. The configuration with the largest baffle diameter, which consistently yields the greatest suppression of velocity, vorticity, and Nusselt number, is then selected as the reference geometry for a subsequent insulation design space analysis. This design space links insulation thermal conductivity and volumetric heat capacity to the convective-to-conductive resistance ratio, enabling a coupled assessment of internal flow control and external insulation properties. Lastly, different baffle geometries, including circular, elliptical, and rectangular rings, are analyzed to determine how shape influences transient flow behavior and thermal resistance. By integrating baffle and insulation design considerations, this study offers a comprehensive framework for enhancing thermal performance in LH2 storage systems.

In this study, a spherical cryogenic tank is modeled in a two-dimensional setup in order to reduce computation time and simplify the analysis. As such, symmetry is assumed in the azimuthal direction,
∂ / ∂ ϕ = 0
(see Fig. 1 ), so that the temperature and flow distribution are only a function of radius r and polar angle θ . The tank is at a 50 % fill level, with the gaseous and liquid hydrogen occupying the upper and lower domains, respectively. A 50 % fill level is selected for two primary reasons: (1) it introduces geometric symmetry between the liquid and vapor phases, which enhances numerical convergence during transient simulations, and (2) it aligns with experimental data from NASA’s 9-inch spherical LH2 tank studies, enabling direct model validation under matched boundary and initial conditions, as shown in Section 2.3.

(1) establishes an initial temperature gradient in the tank insulation layer that couples the saturation temperature of the working fluid with the ambient temperature outside the tank. Piping and plumbing into the tank as well as venting are excluded in this model. The numerical analysis covers early heating behavior under weakly compressible flow conditions, where density variations arise solely from temperature changes, while pressure is decoupled from the energy equation. The model assumes laminar flow conditions throughout the simulation domain, as buoyancy-driven natural convection in cryogenic hydrogen tanks typically occurs in a laminar regime at the length scales and thermal gradients considered here.

If the vorticity is greater than zero, the fluid elements rotate around some axis, in which high vorticity regions indicate (shear) boundary layers, turbulence, and vortex structures in the flow.

The parameters of interest listed here are averaged over the time and spatial domains in order to provide an effective, steady-state approximation of thermal resistance to extract meaningful trends regarding the baffle effects. The thermal resistances,
R

and

, represent the bulk conduction and convective boundary layer resistances, respectively; interfacial or contact resistances between layers are not explicitly modeled and are assumed negligible due to continuous material interfaces and dominant macroscopic transport behavior.

Phase change phenomena are neglected in this study to isolate the effects of natural convection and thermal stratification. Although the system experiences notable thermal gradients and non-negligible inward heat flux, as reflected by elevated Nusselt numbers, the simulation timescale and thermal input are not sufficient to induce significant vaporization or condensation. The working fluid begins at saturation conditions, and local temperature rises are modest due to the high heat capacity of liquid hydrogen. Furthermore, the energy added to the system predominantly contributes to thermal layering and convective transport rather than phase transition. The validity of this assumption is supported by prior experimental and numerical studies focused on convection-dominated regimes in cryogenic liquids. For example, Daney et al. [33] experimentally investigated natural convection in enclosed cryogenic systems under conditions that suppressed phase change entirely, while Lin et al. [34] performed a numerical study of thermal stratification in cryogenic tanks and decoupled the liquid domain from the vapor phase by neglecting interfacial mass and heat transfer. Accordingly, the liquid-gas interface in the present work was resolved using the level set method [35] without coupling to latent heat transfer mechanisms.

COMSOL Multiphysics® is employed for thermofluidic analysis, utilizing 14 Intel Core i9–13900H processors for computation. The numerical solution of the temperature (scalar) and velocity (vector) fields are obtained using the finite element method, where quadratic Lagrange interpolating polynomials discretize the governing equations on a triangular mesh. The mesh refinement level is selected to balance computational efficiency and solution accuracy. The level set approach is implemented to accurately simulate multiphase flow within the cryogenic hydrogen tank, effectively tracking the interface between liquid and vapor hydrogen. To initialize the simulation, a stationary fully-coupled solver is used to establish a stable starting condition. For time-dependent computations, a segregated solver is employed to enhance convergence and efficiency when solving coupled multiphysics equations. Both solvers utilize the Newton-Raphson method to handle the nonlinear system of equations, ensuring numerical stability and rapid convergence.

To apply the Newton-Raphson method for solving a given function
f ( u )
, the goal is to find u such that
f ( u ) = 0
. With an initial guess
u 0
,
f (
u 0
)
and
f ′
(
u 0
)
is found, producing
u 1
; however, if
f (
u 1
) ≠ 0
, another iteration is carried out, until a solution is eventually found in the least number of iterations possible. In highly non-linear problems such as compressible two-phase flow, a damping factor may be used to ensure solution convergence. In situations in which the iterative step is too large,
|

)

, the damping factor provides a way for the numerical solver to restart at a point

(19)

, where α is the damping factor [36]. For this study, the initial damping factor is set between
10
− 4
and 1 for the stationary solver, while the average damping factor is set to 0.8 for the time-dependent solver.

Validation of the numerical model took a two-pronged approach: (1) comparison with experimental data to serve as a benchmark, and (2) comparison between different mesh refinement levels to ensure grid independence. To benchmark the thermal behavior, the model was compared against experimental data from a 1967 NASA study on 9-inch diameter spherical liquid hydrogen tanks [37], following the approach outlined by Li et al. [38], who numerically replicated those results at 50 % fill level and 1 atm. In our study, we extracted temperature data at the same fill level and initial/boundary conditions, focusing on two representative probe locations: R5 in the gas region and R12 in the liquid region, as shown in Fig. 3 a. These correspond to locations used in both the NASA experiments and the validation study by Li et al. From the temperature data provided in Li et al., a regression equation was derived to estimate the initial temperature field. A uniform heat flux of
205
W ·
m
− 2
was applied through an evacuated jacket region, consistent with the Case B scenario outlined in Li et al.’s study [38]. This setup replicates the boundary conditions used in the vacuum-jacketed spherical tank experiments conducted at NASA [37], which serve as the experimental basis for Li et al.’s model validation.

As shown in Fig. 3b, the simulation results capture the trend and magnitude of temperature rise at R5 and R12, with the liquid probe R12 showing a maximum deviation of <1 K and the gas probe R5 exhibiting an average 9 % deviation relative to the experimental data. This level of deviation is consistent with previous numerical studies, such as Li et al. [38], who reported gas-phase errors ranging from 13–34 % due to the inherently unstable and stratified nature of the vapor region. One contributing factor to this deviation is the use of weakly compressible flow modeling, which permits density variation with temperature while assuming a nearly constant pressure field, a valid approximation when flow velocities remain much lower than the speed of sound. In a fully compressible system, such as the NASA experimental tank, the pressure rise contributes to thermodynamic work, diverting part of the heat input into increasing pressure rather than solely raising temperature. The absence of this effect in the weakly compressible model likely causes the gas-phase region (R5) to increase in temperature more rapidly in the simulation compared to the physical experiment. Nevertheless, the approximate agreement in both the temporal slope and final temperature at these probes confirms the model’s ability to capture key thermal behaviors observed in physical experiments, while also illustrating the trade-off between physical fidelity and numerical efficiency in long-duration simulations.

To ensure grid independence, a mesh sensitivity analysis was performed using three levels of refinement: Normal (hauto = 5), Fine (hauto = 4), and Finer (hauto = 3). In COMSOL Multiphysics®, the mesh size is controlled by an integer parameter called hauto, which ranges from 1 (Extremely Fine) to 9 (Extremely Coarse) and sets key parameters such as maximum element size, curvature resolution, and growth rate. Lower hauto values generate finer meshes with higher resolution near boundaries and curved features. As shown in Fig. 4 a and 4b, thermal quantities such as the inner wall heat flow rate and hydrogen temperature exhibit strong agreement across all mesh levels over time. Additionally, local flow variables such as velocity and vorticity magnitude (see Fig. 4c and Fig. 4d) also show convergence after the initial transient period. Minor variations observed in these quantities are typical of buoyancy-driven flows with steep gradients and low Prandtl number fluids. The convergence of both thermal and momentum metrics confirms that the numerical results can be considered mesh-independent. Consequently, the Normal mesh setting was used for all baffle design simulations without compromising the physical fidelity of the model.

This section explores the thermofluidic impact of various baffle configurations within a cryogenic hydrogen storage tank. The analysis proceeds in three stages: first, Section 3.1 evaluates how baffle size influences flow dynamics and convective heat transfer; second, Section 3.2 introduces an insulation design space to assess how baffles interact with varying insulation properties to modulate the convective-to-conductive resistance ratio; and third, Section 3.3 compares different baffle geometries to determine their effectiveness in suppressing convection and enhancing thermal resistance. Together, these subsections present a comprehensive evaluation of how baffle design can be leveraged to improve cryogenic tank performance.

The effectiveness of baffles in modulating convective heat transfer within cryogenic hydrogen storage tanks is strongly influenced by their size. To systematically assess this effect, this section investigates how varying baffle diameters can impact flow dynamics and thermal transport over time. Three baffle configurations with diameters of 1 m (Fig. 5 a), 1.5 m (Fig. 5b), and 2 m (Fig. 5c) are positioned equidistantly within the upper vapor domain, where velocity gradients are highest. The baffle rings are assumed to be supported either by attachment to the outer wall or a central rod (consistent with the azimuthal symmetry of the system) using thin, evenly spaced struts along the azimuthal direction. These struts negligibly influence the overall thermofluidic behavior within the tank. By analyzing velocity magnitude, vorticity distribution, and Nusselt number evolution over a 1200-second transient simulation, the impact of baffle size on flow stabilization and heat transfer suppression was quantified. The results provide insight into the optimal balance between increasing thermal resistance and minimizing disruptive flow effects that could enhance convective mixing.

The baffles in this study are composed of stainless-steel due to their high strength, corrosion resistance, and thermal stability at cryogenic temperatures [39–41]. The insulation layer is composed of two thin stainless-steel shells, one at
r
i n n e r
with a thickness of
r 2
−
r
i n n e r
= 0.1
m
, and another at
r
o u t e r
, with a thickness of
r
o u t e r
−
r 3
= 0.1
m
. A layer of silicon dioxide hollow microspheres is placed between the stainless-steel shells, with a thickness of
r 3
−
r 2
= 1.05
m
. To determine the effective thermal conductivity and volumetric heat capacity as a function of temperature, the parallel, series, and effective medium theory approaches are applied, as detailed in the Supplementary Information. While conduction therefore dominates in the present framework, the values used represent typical effective conductivities, within which radiative contributions may still be significant under high vacuum conditions [42,43].

To determine the effect of the baffle size on flow dynamics and heat transfer, the velocity magnitude, vorticity magnitude, and Nusselt number, averaged across the liquid-gas domain over time, are found for each baffle configuration. The numerical model is run for 1200 s to capture the initial heating behavior within the tank. As shown in Fig. 6 a, the configuration with no baffle results in the highest velocity magnitude on average across the simulation run. As the baffle diameter increases, the velocity magnitude drops significantly across the 1200-second time interval. When averaged over both the time and spatial domains, the velocity magnitude of the
d = 1
m
configuration is 5 % lower than the no-baffle case, while the
d = 1.5
m
and
d = 2
m
configurations exhibit reductions of 33 % and 44 %, respectively. Similarly, as shown in Fig. 6b, as the baffle diameter size increases, the vorticity magnitude drops noticeably across all time values. When averaged over both the time and spatial domains, the vorticity magnitude of the
d = 1
m
configuration is 21 % higher than the no-baffle case, while the
d = 1.5
m
and
d = 2
m
configurations show reductions of 12 % and 17 %, respectively. This indicates that the
d = 2
m
configuration provides superior performance in reducing velocity and vorticity within the tank.

Contrary to the expected trend, the smallest baffle exhibits higher vorticity than the no-baffle configuration. This can be attributed to boundary layer formation along the baffle surface in response to an external flow field induced by natural convection at the inner tank wall. As flow passes over the circular ring baffle, a thin boundary layer develops, producing strong velocity gradients near the surface. While the classical Blasius solution is derived for laminar flow over a flat plate with zero pressure gradient, it provides a useful scaling approximation here because the flow over the baffle is externally imposed and the local curvature is large compared to the boundary layer thickness. The Blasius solution provides a more suitable scaling approximation compared to Ostrach’s solution for natural convection since the baffle is not a source of buoyancy but instead immersed in an externally generated velocity field. Accordingly, the boundary layer along the baffle can be treated as a forced-convection-like layer, where its thickness governs the shear and resulting vorticity, even though the driving flow has a buoyant origin.

The boundary layer thickness as described by the Blasius solution is related to the baffle diameter and Reynolds number as follows:

This explains why larger baffles induce greater flow blockage, slowing the fluid and allowing boundary layers to thicken, which reduces velocity gradients. For the larger baffle rings (

Therefore, the thinner boundary layer results in higher vorticity that at certain time ranges exceeds that of the no-baffle configuration.

is the weighted average thermal conductivity of the gaseous and liquid hydrogen at its saturation temperature. Over the 1200 s simulation run as the hydrogen is heated above its saturation temperature, its thermal conductivity only varies by approximately 1–2 %; as such, it is considered to be approximately constant when calculating the Nusselt number.

The spatially averaged Nusselt number is calculated at each time step using quantities obtained from the simulation environment. Specifically, the total inward heat transfer into the gas-liquid domain is computed via a surface integration of the heat flux along the inner wall (as shown in the numerator of Eq. (27)), which COMSOL interprets as a true 3D surface integral in an axisymmetric geometry. This integrated value is then normalized by the corresponding inner surface area A . The fluid temperature
T
H 2
( t )
is calculated as a volume-averaged value over the gas-liquid domain, and the inner wall temperature
T
i n n e r
( t )
is computed as a surface-averaged value along the wall. Both averages are obtained by normalizing the integrated values by the total fluid volume and wall surface area, respectively. This approach reflects COMSOL’s treatment of 2D axisymmetric domains as fully 3D volumes through rotational symmetry. Together, these spatially averaged quantities yield a domain-averaged Nusselt number.

As shown in Fig. 7 , the no-baffle configuration exhibits the highest Nusselt number
N u
during the initial heating period (
t < 500
s
). This is due to unrestricted natural convection and bulk fluid motion, which drive strong convective heat transfer. However, without any mechanism to sustain vorticity,
N u
for the no-baffle case continues to decline and eventually plateaus as the flow stabilizes. The introduction of baffles, however, leads to contrasting behavior. When averaged over both the time and spatial domain compared to the no-baffle configuration,
N u
for the
d = 1
m
configuration is 2 % lower, the
d = 1.5
m
configuration is 14 % lower, and the
d = 2
m
configuration is 16 % lower. Thus, the lowest Nusselt number is obtained for the largest diameter baffle,
d = 2
m
, indicating significantly reduced convective heat transfer within the liquid-gas hydrogen domain. While the
d = 1
m
baffle configuration initially performs similarly to the larger baffles, its effect on vorticity becomes dominant at later times (
t > 800 s
). Due to thinner boundary layers, it sustains high vorticity levels, leading to enhanced mixing and convective heat transfer. As a result,
N u
for the
d = 1
m
configuration eventually surpasses that of the no-baffle case. In contrast, the larger baffles,
d = 2
m
and
d = 1.5
m
, act as stronger barriers to fluid motion. This reduces large-scale recirculation and suppresses velocity (see Eq. (13)) and vorticity generation, leading to thicker thermal boundary layers and less efficient heat transfer over time. As a result, these configurations maintain the lowest
N u
values throughout the simulation.

It is important to address the relatively high Nusselt number values (
∼
10
4
) observed in this study. These elevated Nusselt numbers result from specific physical factors inherent to the transient natural convection scenario modeled here. Specifically, the large tank radius (7.85 m), the small temperature difference (
∼
10
− 2
K
) between the tank wall and fluid, and the low thermal conductivity (
∼
10
− 2
W ·
m
− 1
K
− 1
) of cryogenic hydrogen significantly amplify the convective heat transfer coefficient during the initial transient heating period (1200 s). Additionally, the large initial temperature differential between the cryogenic hydrogen (initially at 20.369 K) and the ambient environment (300 K) drives substantial heat fluxes into the working fluid. While these Nusselt numbers may appear elevated compared to typical steady-state natural convection benchmarks, from a heat transfer perspective, they reflect physical behavior consistent with the extreme thermal gradients and early transient conditions examined. Over the time domain of 1200 s, the Nusselt number decreases by nearly an order of magnitude (
∼
10
3
), indicating a rapid transition towards steady-state conditions commonly reported in the literature. Similar elevated Nusselt numbers under strongly transient cryogenic conditions have been reported or implied in specialized literature addressing cryogenic tank heat transfer [33,34,44].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Baffle effect on thermal insulation design space
ce:section.ce:para:
The thermal performance of cryogenic hydrogen tanks is governed by the interplay between convective resistance within the working fluid and conductive resistance through the tank insulation layer. While the effectiveness of insulation materials in minimizing heat ingress has been extensively studied, the impact of internal flow modifications, such as baffle configurations, on the convective-to-conductive thermal resistance ratio remains less explored. This section examines how baffles influence thermal resistance in cryogenic tanks by evaluating the ratio of convective-to-conductive resistance as a function of insulation thermal conductivity and volumetric heat capacity, which helps identify the optimal combination of insulation materials and internal flow control mechanisms. To facilitate the discussion, results are presented in a design space format as depicted in Fig. 8 , where calculated convective-to-conductive thermal resistance ratio values are plotted as a function of the tank insulation volumetric heat capacity, parametrized by insulation thermal conductivity. To ensure relevance to practical cryogenic applications, the insulation thermal conductivity and volumetric heat capacity ranges were carefully defined. Common cryogenic insulation materials, such as MLI, aerogels, hollow microspheres, and polyurethane foams, exhibit thermal conductivities ranging from
0.001
W ·
m
− 1
K
− 1
to
0.1
W ·
m
− 1
K
− 1
and volumetric heat capacities from
1
×
10
4
J ·
m
− 3
K
− 1
to
1
×
10
6
J ·
m
− 3
K
− 1
, with
1
×
10
7
J ·
m
− 3
K
− 1
representing an upper bound used to capture extreme or composite material cases [45–50]. This range was selected to parametrize the design space for this work.

The ratio of convective-to-conductive resistance ratio can be expressed by combining Eqs. (15,16):
(29)

(29) is the inverse Biot number, a key dimensionless parameter used when assessing the validity of the lumped capacitance approach [51]. Another significant parameter is the thermal diffusivity of the insulation, defined as:
(31)

Where

is the volumetric heat capacity of the insulation. Thermal diffusivity determines how fast a temperature disturbance, i.e. heating or cooling, propagates through a material, comparing the rate of thermal transport in a material to the rate of thermal energy storage in a material. As shown in Fig. 8, increasing the volumetric heat capacity of the insulation results in more thermal storage compared to thermal transport, increasing the conductive resistance relative to the convective resistance. However, if the thermal conductivity of the insulation increases, the conductive resistance decreases relative to the convective resistance.

In order to quantify the impact of introducing a baffle configuration to the vapor domain in the cryogenic hydrogen tank, the circular ring baffle configuration with diameter
d = 2
m
is selected (Fig. 5c), given its superior performance compared to smaller baffle sizes. For thermal conductivity values
0.001
W ·
m
− 1
K
− 1
(Fig. 9 a),
0.005
W ·
m
− 1
K
− 1
(Fig. 9b),
0.01
W ·
m
− 1
K
− 1
(Fig. 9c), and
0.05
W ·
m
− 1
K
− 1
(Fig. 9d), the resistance ratio from the no-baffle to baffle configuration increases by 30 %, 18 %, 16 %, and 4 %, respectively, when averaged over the volumetric heat capacity domain. Visual inspection of Fig. 9 illustrates how the inclusion of the circular ring baffles (i.e. the dashed line data) for each thermal conductivity leads to an increase in the resistance ratio. Although the introduction of a baffle configuration shifts the total thermal resistance towards convection, the extent of this increase diminishes as thermal conductivity rises. The most significant drop in the resistance ratio occurs when the thermal conductivity increases from
0.01
W ·
m
− 1
K
− 1
to
0.05
W ·
m
− 1
K
− 1
. In this case, the resistance ratio increase drops from 16 % to 4 % when the baffle is introduced to the tank.

This diminishing effect of the baffle configuration increasing the resistance ratio with increasing insulation thermal conductivity is due to the inherent relationship between conduction and convection in the cryogenic hydrogen tank. As insulation thermal conductivity
k
i n s
increases, conductive heat transfer through the insulation layer also increases, allowing more thermal energy to reach the working fluid. This in turn enhances buoyancy-driven convection within the gas domain, amplifying fluid motion and increasing convective heat transfer. The presence of baffles mitigates buoyancy-driven convection by disrupting circulation, but as conductive heat transfer becomes the dominant mode of transport (i.e. increasing
R
c o n v
/
R
c o n d
ratio), the relative impact of convective suppression is reduced.

For
k
i n s
< 0.05
W ·
m
− 1
K
− 1
, the ratio of the velocity magnitude
v
b a f f l e
/ v
(Fig. 10 a) and vorticity magnitude
ω
b a f f l e
/ ω
(Fig. 10b) for the baffle-to-no-baffle case show minimal sensitivity to changes in volumetric heat capacity. However, for
k
i n s
= 0.05
W ·
m
− 1
K
− 1
,
v
b a f f l e
/ v
and
ω
b a f f l e
/ ω
increase by 96 % and 122 %, respectively, with decreasing insulation volumetric heat capacity. Given that decreasing the volumetric heat capacity reduces thermal energy storage, the thermal diffusivity
α
i n s
subsequently increases, which is equivalent to the thermal conductivity
k
i n s
increasing for a given volumetric heat capacity
(
ρ
C p
)
i n s
. This leads to greater fluid motion and rotation that enhances buoyancy-driven convective heat transfer, as detailed in Eqs. (3,6,13).

Overall, while the baffle increases the convective-to-conductive resistance ratio for all insulation thermal conductivity and volumetric heat capacity pairs, its effectiveness is most pronounced at low-to-moderate insulation thermal conductivities for the range of volumetric heat capacities investigated in this study. This occurs because conduction is sufficiently suppressed at these low-to-moderate conductivities, allowing any enhancement in convective resistance to have a noticeable impact, thereby significantly increasing the total resistance of the system. Conversely, at higher thermal conductivities, the increasing dominance of conduction results in a smaller net effect of convective suppression via baffles, underscoring the interdependence between insulation material selection and internal flow modifications for optimizing cryogenic tank thermal performance.

Given that flow separation and boundary layer behavior depend not only on size but also on shape, it is essential to examine how different baffle geometries impact thermal transport and flow stability. This section investigates the effects of distinct baffle geometries on the velocity field, vorticity distribution, and convective heat transfer characteristics. By comparing their performance over time, the effectiveness of different baffle geometries in inhibiting convective flow and reducing thermal transport can be determined. The results illustrate how geometric design affects boundary layer development and overall tank thermal resistance.

As done in the previous sections, the stainless-steel ring baffles for each geometric design are positioned equidistantly from one another in the upper vapor domain in order to facilitate uniform flow and temperature distribution. The insulation layer is composed of densely packed hollow microspheres between two stainless-steel shells. The three different baffle geometries are: elliptical ring baffles with the major axis
a = 1.5
m
and the minor axis
b = 0.5
m
(see Fig. 11 a), rectangular (square) ring baffles with side length
s = 1.5
m
(Fig. 11b), and circular ring baffles with diameter
d = 2
m
(Fig. 11c). The circular ring baffle configuration of
d = 2
m
is selected due to its superior performance compared to the
d = 1.5
m
and
d = 1
m
configurations in suppressing convective heat transfer, velocity, and vorticity.

To evaluate the impact of baffle geometry on flow dynamics and heat transfer, the velocity magnitude, vorticity magnitude, and Nusselt number within the liquid-gas hydrogen domain are analyzed for each configuration. The numerical model is run for 1200 s to capture early heating behavior and transient flow evolution within the tank. As shown in Fig. 12 a, the circular ring baffle shows the largest sustained decrease in velocity magnitude of 44 % compared to the no-baffle case when averaged across time and spatial domains. The elliptical baffle configuration presents the second largest decrease in velocity magnitude with a 22 % decrease compared to the no-baffle case. However, the rectangular (square) baffle configuration exhibits only a 3 % decrease, with the velocity magnitude steadily increasing over time and consistently surpassing the no-baffle case at
t > 992
s
. Compared to the no-baffle case at the final time interval,
t = 1200
s
, the velocity magnitudes of the rectangular, elliptical, and circular ring baffles are 107 % larger, 2 % lower, and 30 % lower, respectively. These results indicate that the circular ring baffle shows potential in long-term flow suppression, preventing sustained convective motion within the tank.

Fig. 12b provides further insights into the flow dynamics by evaluating vorticity magnitude, which characterizes local rotational motion and velocity gradients within the fluid (hydrogen). The circular ring baffle exhibits the highest suppression of vorticity, with a reduction of 17 % compared to the no-baffle case when averaged across time and spatial domains, demonstrating its ability to mitigate shear-induced turbulence and inhibit convective transport. Conversely, the elliptical and rectangular (square) baffle configurations show higher vorticity, increasing by 32 % and 78 %, respectively, relative to the no-baffle case. The rectangular ring baffle in particular generates the highest sustained vorticity, exceeding the no-baffle case by 286 % at
t = 1200
s
, compared to the elliptical and circular ring baffles, which exceed the no-baffle case by 88 % and 16 %, respectively, at
t = 1200
s
. The high vorticity observed for the rectangular baffle configuration can be attributed to localized shear effects along the sharp edges of each baffle, which facilitate the formation of small-scale vortices and enhance convective mixing.

The differences in vorticity between curved and sharp-edged baffles can be explained by examining boundary layer theory and the effects of shear stress. The generation of vorticity is closely tied to the velocity gradient in the boundary layer (Eq. (22)), in which higher velocity gradients near the wall result in increased vorticity production. Curved geometries, such as circular and elliptical ring baffles, promote smooth boundary layer development, leading to gradual velocity transitions and lower vorticity generation. Conversely, sharp-edged baffles induce abrupt velocity changes, which significantly increase shear stress at the edges, resulting in thinner boundary layers and high vorticity production in those regions. Eq. (24) highlights how the vorticity and boundary layer thickness are inversely related, indicating that sharp-edged baffles with thin boundary layers induce high vorticity due to the steeper velocity gradient from the wall to the free stream. These results emphasize how baffle geometry plays a crucial role in modulating vorticity and velocity, in which smooth, curved baffles are more effective at suppressing fluid flow by reducing localized shear effects and stabilizing boundary layer development.

Eq. (26) is used to compute the Nusselt number averaged over the spatial domain for each baffle configuration over time (see Fig. 13 ), allowing for the quantification of their effectiveness in reducing convective heat transfer. The circular and elliptical ring baffles display significant suppression of convective heat transfer across the entire time domain. On a time-averaged basis, compared to the no-baffle configuration, the elliptical ring baffle reduces
N u
by 24 %, and the circular ring baffle reduces
N u
by 16 %. In contrast, the rectangular (square) baffle shows no change in
N u
indicating no average change in convective heat transfer over time. For early time intervals,
t < 600
s
, all three baffle configurations exhibit a Nusselt number lower than the no-baffle case, indicating the effectiveness of a generalized baffle configuration in suppressing convective heat transfer.

This reduction in the Nusselt number is directly linked to the velocity magnitude, as shown in Eq. (13), with Fig. 12a illustrating that all three configurations cause comparable and significantly lower velocity magnitudes than the no-baffle scenario. Furthermore, Fig. 12b demonstrates that the vorticity magnitude of the circular ring baffles remains lower than that of the no-baffle case for most of the time domain. In contrast, the vorticity magnitudes of the elliptical and rectangular ring baffles increase substantially at
t
> 1000
s
. Consequently, their corresponding
N u
values increase to a greater extent than those of the circular ring baffle configuration.

At
t = 1200
s
, the
N u
of the rectangular baffle configuration is 35 % higher compared to the no-baffle case. In contrast, it is 2 % lower for the elliptical ring configuration and 7 % lower for the circular ring configuration, suggesting that the latter may provide the most sustained suppression of convective heat transfer over extended time periods. Overall, curved geometries are more effective in reducing thermal transport within the hydrogen domain compared to rectangular geometries. The sharp edges of rectangular geometries generate significant velocity gradients across the boundary layer, resulting in high vorticities and convective mixing that enhance heat transfer over time.

This study demonstrates that the inclusion of baffles in cryogenic hydrogen tanks can significantly suppress convective heat transfer and improve thermal performance. A 2D axisymmetric model was developed using the level set method, with a 50 % fill level and a transient simulation time of 1200 s under weakly compressible conditions. First, the effects of circular ring baffles with different diameters (1 m, 1.5 m, and 2 m) were evaluated relative to the no-baffle case. The 2 m baffle configuration consistently yielded the lowest spatially averaged velocity, vorticity, and Nusselt number over time, establishing it as the optimal configuration. This optimal baffle configuration was then used to explore an insulation design space, examining the relationship between the convective-to-conductive thermal resistance ratio and insulation thermal conductivity and volumetric heat capacity. Results showed that baffle effectiveness depends strongly on insulation properties, with diminished benefits at high insulation diffusivity values. Finally, baffle geometry was varied to evaluate the influence of shape on flow and heat transfer suppression. Curved baffles, particularly the circular ring configuration, demonstrated superior performance compared to rectangular, sharp-edged configurations, offering the greatest reductions in velocity, vorticity, and convective transport.

Regarding baffle size optimization, the results illustrate that larger baffles are more effective in reducing velocity magnitude, vorticity magnitude, and convective heat transfer within the hydrogen domain. Specifically, the largest circular ring baffle configuration of 2 m diameter showed a 44 % reduction in velocity magnitude, 17 % reduction in vorticity magnitude, and 16 % decrease in the Nusselt number compared to the no-baffle case. Moreover, the smallest circular ring baffle size of 1 m diameter showed greater convective heat transfer at later time intervals due to increased vorticity induced by large velocity gradients between the thin boundary layer and free stream.

The insulation design space analysis indicated that the inclusion of circular ring baffles increases the convective-to-conductive resistance ratio between 4 % and 30 %, with the most significant benefits observed at low-to-moderate insulation thermal conductivities. At higher conductivities, conduction becomes the dominant mode of heat transfer, diminishing the impact of baffle-induced flow suppression. Similarly, lower volumetric heat capacities, associated with higher thermal diffusivities, lead to intensified buoyancy-driven convection, thereby reducing the effectiveness of the baffle configuration. Corresponding velocity and vorticity magnitude ratio values further confirmed that baffle performance deteriorates under high-diffusivity conditions, driven by increased fluid motion and steeper thermal gradients.

In addition to size considerations and effects on the insulation performance, baffle geometry also plays a crucial role in modulating heat transfer behavior. The circular ring baffles consistently outperformed the elliptical and rectangular (square) ring baffles, exhibiting the lowest sustained velocity and vorticity magnitudes over the simulation time domain, resulting in the lowest Nusselt number at
t = 1200
s
. The rectangular ring baffles generated the highest vorticity, leading to enhanced convective mixing and increased heat transfer at later times. This behavior is attributed to sharp-edged geometries promoting localized shear effects, which induce high velocity gradients and sustain vortical structures that enhance heat transport. In contrast, the circular and elliptical ring baffles facilitate smoother boundary layer development, reducing velocity gradients and suppressing convective motion. These findings confirm that curved geometries outperform sharp-edged baffles by minimizing convective heat transfer, making them preferable for cryogenic tank applications.

This study offers a comprehensive framework for optimizing baffle configurations in cryogenic hydrogen storage tanks, highlighting their role in suppressing convection and enhancing overall thermal resistance. The results underscore the importance of considering baffle size, geometry, and insulation properties as coupled design variables, rather than isolated components. To maximize thermal performance, in addition to size and geometric shape, effective baffle design must be tailored to the thermal conductivity and heat capacity of the insulation material. Future work may expand this framework by employing three-dimensional modeling to capture azimuthal effects, such as strut-induced flow disturbances, and by exploring a broader range of baffle geometries to refine flow suppression strategies for practical cryogenic systems.
